data.title: BETA Technologies: Amazon Surge Is A False Flag, But Vertical Integration Makes It A Buy
data.description:
BETA Technologies (BETA) stock surged 15.6% following a stake disclosure from Amazon (AMZN) and an upgrade from Jefferies. BETA Technologies is also backed by GE Aerospace (GE), and I believe that the company has a lot of unique drivers that many other developers of eVTOLs simply do not have, and that makes it more attractive for investors, including big corporations such as Amazon and GE Aerospace. In this report, I will discuss these unique drivers and assess the investment case for BETA.

BETA Technologies Amazon Surge Is A False Flag

BETA Technologies went public in November 2025, raising $1 billion in capital. BETA is already substantially different in that regard compared to peers, as it went public through a traditional IPO rather than a SPAC. Since then, the stock price has come down to as low as $15.61 before the rebound to $19.39. BETA Technologies already had quite a strong backing in investor rounds. In September 2025, GE Aerospace announced a $300 million equity investment in BETA through private placement. Amazon’s Climate Pledge Fund already invested in BETA in 2021. The stock now surged on the news that Amazon holds a stake, but this was already disclosed in the prospectus (S-1, Page 153).

We see that both Amazon and GE Aerospace were already listed in the prospectus as parties holding more than 5% ownership of the company. So, the disclosure of Amazon holding 5.3% is not a surprising one, nor does it constitute an additional equity contribution as far as I can tell. It provides investors with a sign of equity and strategic backing from Amazon, but that equity backing could already be seen in the prospectus as well as the press release from Amazon’s Climate Pledge Fund from 2021. So, in my view, it is a bit of a false flag for the company’s stock price to surge.

BETA Has An Attractive Business Setup

BETA Technologies is taking a vastly different approach, which I believe creates a lower-risk capitalization opportunity with substantially more revenue streams compared to peers. The company has two airplane products, which are the ALIA VTOL and the ALIA CTOL. In its effort to bring an eVTOL (electric vertical take-off/landing) airplane to the market, they are taking an intermediate step with the eCTOL (electric conventional take-off/landing) airplane, which is set to enter the market first and can be certified using FAA Part 23 standards, which is the certification standard for normal category airplanes. The CTOL is expected to be certified in late 2026 or early 2027, with certification for the eVTOL 12 months later. So, the company is creating two products from a somewhat unified development framework. Components such as the H500 engine are expected to be certified this year. There always is the risk of schedule delays, but BETA has a stepwise approach, certifying the engines, then the CTOL, and then the VTOL. This is an approach that is more conservative compared to peers and may also mean that the company’s revenue generation is less binary than it is for many of its peers.

The company is also positioning itself as a full-stack electric aviation OEM and propulsion merchant supplier. I believe this is a key differentiator. BETA develops and manufactures the powertrain, batteries, and charging equipment in-house, whereas most eVTOL developers are system integrators doing final assembly sourced from a pool of suppliers. This means that BETA Technologies, compared to peers, should be able to drive substantially more high-margin revenues from the aftermarket since it has the OEM (original equipment manufacturer) expertise on the component level.

Since BETA does more design and manufacturing itself, it can also provide solutions to other eVTOL manufacturers. Since BETA will also be competing with those same eVTOL manufacturers in some sense, integration with other eVTOLs may not be desired and may actually also provide some integration risk. However, overall, the company does have a vertically integrated setup along the energy/power axis of the CTOL/VTOL that will allow it to harvest aftermarket sales more efficiently, and it could also be a supplier, particularly on a unified charging network for eVTOLs.

The company also has a dual-use opportunity, meaning the product can be used for commercial as well as defense purposes. Due to the vertical setup, BETA is already generating revenues from propulsion sales even with certification not being complete yet on the component level, let alone on the aircraft level. Other revenues come from services revenues, small contributions from the charging network, and classified defense work. So, the company is already generating revenues, and we see a 70% gross profit margin. The operating loss is still $80.6 million, but it carries R&D expenses in support of development and certification of the new products.

The company also has a near-term revenue driver through eIPP, which is a government-backed integration pilot program. This could drive revenues by 2026 either from aircraft sales and battery & charging revenues or payments for demonstrations. This would provide a strong entry point for BETA to commence cargo and (medical) logistics flights. Ideally, the CTOL is certified by the time the eIPP program has a real revenue component. However, since this is a pilot program, it could be the case that uncertified airplanes will be allowed to participate under a special type certificate when a certain level of maturity is demonstrated. The earliest revenues from eIPP will not be huge, but it will be a strategically strong position to be able to participate in this program and smooth out some challenges for integration into commercial operations ahead of commercial service entry.

So, overall, the stepped approach and the energy aviation stack approach with in-house component design and manufacturing position BETA Technologies extremely well in the market.

BETA Technologies Is A Speculative Buy

Valuing BETA Technologies is not an easy task. The valuation is highly dependent on which EV/Sales multiple is used along with sales projections being correct as well as cash conversion and EBITDA margins being reasonably correct. I am currently assuming mid-point margins for reasonable margin ranges with a 4% margin for 2028, 11.5% for 2029, and 15% for 2030. We see that the stock is fully valued against an aerospace peer group multiple of 4.3x. However, we also note that emerging companies tend to trade substantially higher. At 2x the peer group multiple, we would see a 96% upside. My current stock price target is based on 1.5x peer group EV/Sales, which coincides with a 6.5x EV/Sales multiple that would indicate 48% upside with a $28.64 price target.

Conclusion: BETA Technologies Has An Extremely Attractive Setup

Names in the eVTOL landscape remain higher risk, and so any buy rating is a speculative buy rating. The buy rating for BETA Technologies is no different. I believe that the company has a very attractive setup as an eVTOL developer with OEM and energy aviation stack capabilities, enabling it to harvest high-margin aftermarket revenues from its own products but also from other parties with whom BETA has contracted for parts. I don’t believe the surge in the stock price is justified if that is driven by the Amazon stake disclosure, because that Amazon had a stake in the company was clear as day. The Jefferies upgrade provides a better reason for the increase in the stock price, and my assessment also shows that if BETA delivers on revenue expectations, substantially higher stock prices can indeed be unlocked.

data.title: VICI Properties Vs. Gaming and Leisure Properties: The Vegas-Focused REIT Is The Winner
data.description:
We recently initiated a new position in VICI Properties (VICI) and shared our investment thesis in an article that you can read by clicking here.

In short, we think that the market has overreacted to the recent slowdown in Las Vegas, not realizing that VICI is a triple net lease landlord with 40-plus-year-long leases that continued to generate steady and predictable income even through the pandemic. Even then, it's now priced at a historically low valuation, and we think that it's a great buy-the-dip opportunity.

This recent article led many of you to ask me why we don't invest in its close peer, Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI), instead, and that's going to be the focus of today's follow-up analysis.

After all, it's trading at an even lower valuation, offering a slightly higher yield, enjoying better diversification, and its smaller size could result in faster growth over time.

VICI GLPI
AFFO Multiple 12.1x 11.3x
Dividend Yield 6.3% 6.9%
Market Cap $31 billion $13 billion

We like GLPI, and my preference for VICI is not significant. I like casino net lease properties a lot and could see myself owning GLPI someday in the future.

You can read more about why we like casino net lease properties by clicking here.

However, the reason why we favor VICI is its greater focus on Las Vegas assets compared to GLPI, which focuses on regional properties.

VICI generates about half of its revenue from the Las Vegas Strip, owning trophy assets such as the Caesars Palace, the Venetian, the MGM Grand, etc., whereas GLPI owns regional casinos such as the Bally’s Casino in Black Hawk or DraftKings at Casino Queen in St. Louis.

VICI - the Venetian, Las Vegas, NV

GLPI - DraftKings at Casino Queen, St. Louis

Some investors argue that GLPI's regional casinos are preferable as they could be more resilient to recessions and allow the REIT to better diversify the portfolio.

That may be true, but it does not tell the full story.

Both have pros and cons, but overall, I think that the regional casinos of GLPI are riskier than the Vegas Strip casinos of VICI for three reasons:

Reason 1: Lower Barriers to Entry

The Las Vegas Strip is the most economically productive street in the entire world. Most of the land is already built out, limiting future potential supply. The properties on the strip also enjoy valuable brands, which is unique in real estate and only further increases the strength of their moats.

The same cannot be said about the regional casinos of GLPI.

They're typically in much worse locations, which puts them at greater risk of facing future competition.

Over the long run, real estate is all about location, location, location… because that’s what protects your asset from future supply and allows it to grow in value over time.

Reason 2: Greater Regulatory Risk

Las Vegas has become a global entertainment hub, attracting people from all over the world for all kinds of entertainment, not just gambling. People visit Vegas all the time to party, watch shows and sports, eat good food, relax, and then gamble a bit. The point is that Vegas does not pull its traffic just for gambling. It's well diversified, which protects it from potential regulatory changes. There's only one Vegas, and it will remain a major entertainment hub far into the future and likely only grow further over time as it keeps diversifying into new things like F1, new sports teams, the Sphere, etc.

This makes VICI's Las Vegas properties far more resilient because they enjoy a constant flow of traffic that comes to Vegas for a lot of different reasons. They then end up staying at its properties, eating out, shopping, and even gambling a bit, despite it often not being the main reason for the trip.

Regional casinos, on the other hand, exist primarily for gambling. Their entertainment options are not nearly as diversified. They're gambling destinations, and that exposes them to much greater regulatory risk. As an example, a given state may decide to increase the number of casino licenses, leading to new competition just across the street. Or it may increase its casino taxes, leading to a steep drop in profitability.

Worse yet, a nearby state may change its regulations to allow casinos in the future. I remember driving from Dallas, Texas, to just across the border to Oklahoma to visit the biggest casino in the world: The WinStar World Casino and Resort.

It's just across the border to attract gamblers from Texas, where casinos are illegal, but what if Texas were to change its regulations in the future? That casino would certainly take a big hit, as most of its visitors go there primarily for gambling and not for other forms of entertainment.

GLPI does not own this specific property, but it owns many others that are in a similar position.

As an example, GLPI owns Hollywood Casino Tunica, which is in Mississippi, right at the border with Arkansas and Tennessee, which have historically had stricter gambling rules. This casino has so far benefited from a lot of traffic coming from the neighboring states, but if their rules were to change and new competition emerged, it could lose some of this business, leading to a permanent loss in value.

This makes GLPI's regional properties a lot less resilient than the average property on the Vegas Strip. They rely primarily on gambling to attract people, and that could be disrupted in the future due to potential regulatory changes opening the door to more competitors.

Reason 3: Digital Cannibalization

Finally, I think that GLPI's regional casinos are also at higher risk of suffering from the growth of online gambling over the long run.

As mentioned earlier, Vegas will thrive because it's far more than gambling. It has become a true entertainment hub. I have gone five times to Vegas, and it was never for gambling. It has always been for sports, conferences, or just having fun with friends. While I'm there, I may gamble a little, but that’s not the primary reason why I visit Vegas. Digital gaming will not change that.

However, since regional casinos are all about gambling, I think that their traffic could be affected if more people switch to online gambling. Now, I understand that the experience is not the same, and one does not directly compete with the other. In fact, casino operators have even shown that digital gaming can benefit their physical casinos, as it introduces new people to gambling who have never set foot in a casino. By following hybrid strategies, they can bring people into their digital ecosystem, have them gamble, and then grant them rewards to be used at physical casinos to increase traffic, for instance.

This works well, and I'm not saying that GLPI's regional casinos will go under because of online gambling. But I do think that there's a long-term risk to it that cannot be overlooked.

Risks to My Thesis

If you think that Las Vegas has entered a structural and long-lasting downturn, then this would, of course, change everything. This could happen, for example, if lots of other similar destinations emerged internationally, leading to greater competition.

In that case, VICI's tenants would likely see their profitability decline, and this could eventually lead to lease defaults and even a dividend cut.

I don't expect that to happen, but if you think that the best days of Vegas are behind it, then you would likely be better off investing in GLPI instead.

Conclusion

In a way, VICI's Las Vegas casinos vs. GLPI's regional casinos are like comparing a Class A mall in a prime location with well-diversified uses, including lots of entertainment, restaurants, offices, and even apartments, to an outlet center in a more remote location with only shopping.

The outlet would be in a riskier situation because of its worse location, and its lack of diversification is exposing it to greater competitive threats from other properties and e-commerce.

This is not to say that the outlet is a bad investment, but it should trade at a discount to compensate for its higher risk.

In the case of VICI and GLPI, the discount is not large enough for me to invest in GLPI instead of VICI. I would much rather pay the small premium to own true trophy assets on the Las Vegas Strip.

Yes, this also means being more concentrated on one location, but that's worthwhile in this particular case. Similarly, I would much rather hold my assets in one bank that’s very solid rather than two weak banks. Diversification just for the sake of diversification does not always make sense.

At High Yield Landlord, we have managed to stay ahead of the REIT market averages over the long run by identifying such differences and capitalizing on those that offer the risk-to-reward to investors.

Our choice is VICI in the casino sector.

data.title: Strong Jobs Market Suggests Considering Cyclical Stocks
data.description:
The first of the labor market reports for 2026 has dropped, and it doesn't disappoint. Unemployment rate is down, non-farm payrolls are up. It doesn't get much better. Except, maybe, for rate sensitive sectors that would have benefited from economic weakness resulting in an easier monetary policy.

Interestingly though, cyclicals aren't doing too badly, and a healthy economy could well be more than sufficient to support them even if no rate cuts happen going forward. But that remains to be seen. All of this is covered in this article.

What the labor market report says

The labor market showed signs of health in January, with the unemployment rate dropping for the second consecutive month, and non-farm payroll increases did exactly the same. The details are below.

  • Unemployment rate

The unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%, which is quite the start for the year considering that it falls below the Fed's target rate of 4.4% this year. It's also the smallest rate seen in the last six months (the figure for October 2025 wasn't released).

It does bear mentioning, though, that the labor market has still weakened over the year, though. In January 2025, the unemployment rate was a notably lower 4%. Still, it's encouraging that runaway weakness hasn't happened, and the number has actually subsided from the highs of November 2025.

  • Non-farm payrolls

Non-farm payrolls increased by 130k in January 2026, the biggest monthly increase in over a year. The last time that the number expanded at an even higher rate was only in December 2024, by 237k.

Not only is the latest figure a positive on a standalone basis, but the fact that non-farm payrolls have now increased for three consecutive months (see chart below) is a relief too. Between May and October 2025, the number contracted in three of the six months.

Rate cut probability drops further

After the FOMC statement of January, the market were already fairly convinced that the Fed is unlikely to cut rates at the March 17-18 meeting. It's now even more so. The probability of a rate cut, based on the 30-day Fed funds futures rates, has dropped to just 7.5% from 13.4% after the last FOMC meeting.

That this probability has been directly impacted by the latest jobs report is evident from the fact that as of February 10, a day before its release, the probability was at a much higher 20%. Of course, it's still pre-mature to predict with certainty what might happen at the meeting, as plenty of macro data points are due before that, including another jobs report and two inflation releases. But so far, the indications of another meeting of unchanged rates is on the cards.

Stock market response

During the earnings season, it's harder to assess the impact of macroeconomic events on headline indices and the latest stock market response is no different. At yesterday's market close, the indices weren't notably moved to start with. And to the extent that they were, there wasn't harmony in their direction.

While the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (QQQ) made some gains, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) was essentially flat, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was marginally down (see table below).

The more interesting trend is visible YTD. The Dow Jones has made the biggest gains, followed by the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ 100 is actually marginally in the red. Since the time of the previous jobs report, only DIA has made any real progress.

A year of cyclicals?

These YTD trends for key indices appear to indicate that this could well be the year of cyclicals. Consider this. The biggest five holdings for the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA) are the financial services company Goldman Sachs (GS), construction and mining equipment provider Caterpillar (CAT), Microsoft (MSFT), home improvement retailer Home Depot (HD) and paints company Sherwin-Williams (SHW).

Four of these five are cyclical stocks, with Microsoft being the exception. There's more. Take a look at the chart below. Three of these five holdings have seen double digit percentage gains YTD. One, which is Goldman Sachs, has seen single digit gains, while Microsoft is the only one to have seen a double-digit percentage drop so far this year. In other words, all except the non-cyclical technology stock have seen gains in 2026 so far.

This raises the question if 2026 could well be the year of cyclicals. The latest labor market report's robustness certainly suggests so. Especially when seen in conjunction with expectations of healthy GDP growth and muted inflation this year.

A look at the sectoral trends offers no clear conclusions, at least not yet. Sectors like consumer discretionary and financial services are still weak YTD, even as others like materials and energy have seen strength, as reflected in the table below.

This leaves the question - where should an investment be made now? One option to consider would be the DIA itself. Except that the index's P/E isn't supportive, at 23.2x, as it's slightly higher than the average of 22.6x over the past five years, implying that the index could stay basically flat going forward.

A look at the ratings for DIA's biggest holdings also shows no clear buying opportunity. In fact, interestingly, Microsoft is the only one that stands out the most. Not only is it rated a Buy by Seeking Alpha analysts but also a Strong Buy by Wall Street. The point here is, right now is a rare wait and watch moment on what to buy next.

In conclusion

It might be a wait and watch, but it's a time of active waiting. Opportunities are likely to arise, if the labor markets are any indication. And most likely, they will be seen in cyclical stocks, even if no interest rate cuts happen. Even more specifically, segments like materials and industrials are worth tracking as superior performers YTD.

This could change of course, with any big policy changes or a turn in macroeconomic trends. But for now, the year appears to be tilting in favour of cyclicals.

data.title: Angi Q4: Trading At 5x FCF As Demand Remains Under Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Over the past several quarters, Angi Inc. (ANGI) has embarked on a business transformation aimed at fundamentally reshaping how it matches customers with service professionals (or Pros). A key change involved giving customers the option to choose a specific pro instead of auto-matching. This has resulted in drastically improved NPS and churn rates, alongside significantly higher profitability and FCF generation. However, this has been achieved at the cost of shrinking the overall size of the business, with revenue down over 25% from 2023 levels. This is particularly important as Angi is a marketplace where scale and network effects are vital to the health of the platform. In my previous write-up, I argued that management was taking the right actions, even if it meant taking two steps back before moving forward. The valuation had gotten too cheap, given the line of sight for a return to mid to high single-digit topline growth.

Unfortunately, Q4 results reveal that after taking two steps back, the company is unable to demonstrate its ability to move forward. Growth expectations have now been tempered, and while management remains optimistic and the stock is cheaply valued at just 5.3 times FCF, I do not find the investment case compelling enough relative to peers, especially amid the uncertainty around the impact from LLMs. As a result, I am downgrading shares to a Hold.

Concerns Arising From Q4 Results

Angi's Q4 revenue fell 10% year-over-year to $241 million, missing expectations. This resulted in Adjusted EBITDA for FY25 coming in at $140 million, at the bottom of the guidance range. While Q4 results were weak, the more negative development was management's conservative approach to full-year guidance. While revenue through its Proprietary channel grew 23% year-over-year in Q4, this momentum is expected to slow due to pressure from Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) SEO. Meanwhile, the much smaller Network channel declined 79% in Q4 and is expected to continue to face modest declines in the coming quarters. This has led to full-year revenue growth expectations being reduced to the single digits from the prior expectation for mid to high single digits. The decline in the Network channel has largely been intentional, as management shifted toward higher-quality lead generation, and was previously expected to stabilize starting in Q1.

Despite the muted topline growth expectations, management is guiding for Adjusted EBITDA to grow to between $145 to $150 million while lowering capex from $60 million to $50 million. Management also aims to significantly increase marketing spend to increase brand awareness and lift revenue earned through its proprietary channel, which includes TV and social media, with lower dependency on SEO. This increased spend is to be funded by cost savings from last month's restructuring, expected to total $70 million to $80 million on an annualized basis.

While it is encouraging to see that the organization is becoming more efficient, investors also need to see the business return to solid topline growth. Management expects to achieve this for FY26 through a combination of mid-single-digit growth in the proprietary channel and stabilization of the declines in the network channel. With a modest revenue decline expected for Q1, the market remains skeptical regarding management's more positive outlook for the remaining quarters.

Turning to the supply side, key metrics related to the Pros on the platform shown above continue to decline. While management argues that the capacity and revenue per Pro are up, it is concerning to see this trend persist, particularly as maintaining a balance between supply and demand is essential for efficient platform operation. While actions are being taken to reverse these declines by bringing on both small and large Pros onto the platform, the progress here is yet to show up in the numbers.

Valuation and Potential Upside Drivers

Following the decline post-earnings, with shares now at $9.2, Angi's market cap is $370 million based on 40.1 million shares outstanding. Its enterprise value stands at $564 million when considering its cash position of $304 million and long-term debt of $498 million. With management's FY26 guidance calling for $147.5 million in adjusted EBITDA, I see FCF landing at $70.5 million after deducting $12 million in net interest expenses, $50 million in annual capex, and $15 million in tax expenses. This translates to a price-to-FCF multiple of just 5.3.

While the stock appears very cheap, I would point out that several other marketplace businesses with similar scale and growth expectations, such as Yelp Inc. (YELP), Fiverr International Ltd. (FVRR), and Etsy, Inc. (ETSY), are currently trading at only modest premiums. In Angi's case, I would argue that a valuation discount is warranted due to structural differences in customer retention dynamics, where it's easier for future transactions to be moved off-platform, thereby lowering the lifetime value of its customers.

Turning to some positive aspects that could drive outperformance relative to these estimates, this hinges on the marketing spend successfully driving customers onto the platform. If this is coupled with earlier than expected stabilization in the Network channel, there is room for growth to surprise to the upside. On the Pros side, a ramp-up in online enrollments, which require minimal incremental costs, could bring more smaller Pros easily onto the platform, helping reverse the current trend and improving the supply-demand dynamic.

Risks

Needless to say, management's reliance on driving growth through increased marketing comes with risks attached to the spend, not driving the desired return. The home services space remains highly competitive with formidable competitors such as Thumbtack and Yelp, among others.

As highlighted above, Angi's International business has struggled to generate growth, with revenue down 2% year-over-year in Q4 and for the full year. This is despite the International segment not facing the headwind from lower Network channel revenue that is impacting the U.S. Looking ahead, similar growth challenges could emerge in the U.S. if the planned marketing initiatives fail to gain traction.

Another key risk is related to how Angi interacts with LLMs to drive traffic to its site. While they did recently partner with Amazon.com, Inc.'s (AMZN) Alexa and stated during the earnings call that they are actively involved with another major LLM, it is noteworthy that these partnerships are unlikely to be exclusive and will likely involve Angi sacrificing its margins. Angi argues that its nationwide database of pros and the ability to use search context from the customer to make the right match effectively positions it as a system of record, thereby giving it the right to win. While management referred to being the system of record multiple times during the call, I remain uncertain about the role that Angi will have with these LLM agents.

While its balance sheet consists of $196 million in net debt, this is less concerning given the FCF generation from the business and the fact that the debt is not due until August 2028. Moreover, with buybacks now paused following the completion of the program, cash levels are likely to rise in the coming quarters until management is permitted under post-spin-off regulations to start a new program.

Downgrading ANGI Stock to a Hold

Although the stock appears cheap following the post-earnings drop, the outlook remains uncertain and carries execution risk. While cost reductions have improved near-term margins, a return to topline growth is crucial for driving investor returns from here. I think it is prudent to maintain a neutral stance while awaiting clear signs that the business has returned to sustainable organic growth before I turn more positive on the stock.

data.title: Separating The Winners And Losers Of The AI Disruption
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Jobs report: There's a lot of discussion going on after non-farm payrolls shock to the upside. See expectations about rate cuts and the SA analyst reaction roundup.

All about energy: The Trump administration is seeking to boost coal with new contracts, while appealing court losses on offshore wind rulings.

Drone sightings: The U.S. Army deployed AeroVironment's (AVAV) LOCUST laser weapon system near the El Paso International Airport, leading to a seven-hour airspace shutdown.

Navigating the shift

Believers in the SaaSpocalypse claimed their latest casualty on Wednesday, sending shares of real estate services deep into the red. Names like CBRE Group (CBRE), Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL), and Cushman & Wakefield (CWK) all tanked by more than 12%, marking their biggest daily drop since the COVID pandemic. Whether the sudden selloff will last remains to be seen, but the pace of the AI rollout and sharp shift in sentiment continue to surprise many on Wall Street. We’re entering a ‘very violent technology cycle’ driven by AI

Snapshot: The Software-as-a-Service industry, or SaaS stocks, have been under pressure since the beginning of the year, but there's a growing list of sub-sectors that are getting punished. Last week, Anthropic released Claude Cowork, which automates professional workflows in the legal and financial sectors, leading to plunges in shares like LegalZoom (LZ) and QuickBooks provider Intuit (INTU). Earlier this week, wealth manager stocks got slammed following the release of Altruist's tax planning tool called Hazel, leading to declines for LPL Financial (LPLA), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Ameriprise (AMP), and Raymond James (RJF).

The theme here is disruption, with these high-fee businesses considered vulnerable to new AI tools and automation. It could ultimately hit their bottom lines, leading to a compression of fees and margins, and eventually cost jobs when zooming out to the macroeconomy. Other pockets of the AI trade have been doing quite well, like the firms that are offering these artificial intelligence applications, data centers, or the chips making the revolution possible, but the question remains over traditional models and to what extent software will be upended.

Buckle up: "Software is not on the verge of obsolescence. Rather, AI is widening the gap between winners and losers, making outcomes more asymmetric," Neuberger Berman writes in Software Leads AI-Driven Tech Rout - What Next? "Some incumbents will falter, but others will adapt—integrating AI, innovating and strengthening competitive positions—creating selective opportunities for investors who can differentiate. Indiscriminate selling can create opportunity, but only for businesses that are fundamentally sound and demonstrably capable of adapting. The near-term objective should be to position for transition rather than extinction; this can be done by emphasizing resilience in business models and flexibility in balance sheets, as periods of structural change tend to punish fragility." (3 comments)

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Inside HDO's Strategy For A Changing Market

Amazon Hit The Jackpot With Anthropic

8%+ Dividends: 2 Retirement Income Powerhouses

Nvidia: Ready To Impress (Earnings Preview)

The Smart Money Is Buying REITs, These Could Be Next

What else is happening...

Amazon Pharmacy (AMZN) to bring same-day delivery to 4,500 cities.

Meta's (META) auditor EY raises red flag over data center accounting.

Elon Musk: xAI (X.AI) needs to build AI satellite factory on the moon.

Robinhood (HOOD) remains bullish on crypto, but continues to diversify.

CME (CME) weighs launching first-ever rare earth futures contract.

What is next for Disney (DIS) after its Q1 earnings, succession plan?

No plans for Chipotle (CMG) to offer McDonald's-style value menu.

Kraft Heinz (KHC) pauses separation to invest in growth and profits.

Worsening picture: CBO raises 10-year U.S. deficit outlook by $1.4T.

House votes to end Canada tariffs as six Republicans defy Trump.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan flat. Hong Kong -0.9%. China +0.1%. India -0.7%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.1%. Paris +0.7%. Frankfurt +1.2%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.3%. S&P +0.3%. Nasdaq +0.3%. Crude -0.1% to $64.56. Gold -0.3% to $5,084.50. Bitcoin +1.7% to $68,104.
Ten-year Treasury Yield unchanged at 4.18%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Nebius (NBIS) and Coinbase (COIN).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Lucid: Not Yet A Buy, But Selling Options (For A High Yield) Makes Sense
data.description:
One company that I’ve had on my radar for a while is Lucid Group (LCID), the luxury EV manufacturer and competitor to leading brands like Tesla (TSLA) and Rivian (RIVN).

While the company lacks the scale or name-brand recognition of these competitors, I believe that the business has a credible path to structural profitability and long-term returns.

What’s the catch?

In short, the stock has been smoked since coming public in 2021.

Shares are down 90% from the SPAC price and more than 98% from all-time highs, performance that reflects broad market skepticism in the company’s high burn rate, and share sales that have inflated the diluted share count by more than 150%:

So far, Lucid has not been a good investment.

However, as the company’s upfront investments begin to pay off, deliveries and revenue continue to surge, and new partnerships are announced, it seems as though the firm is starting to stand on solid ground.

To be clear, I don’t expect that Lucid is anywhere close to turning the corner on profitability. That would require considerable future investments in scale and significantly higher volumes. However, it seems as though the company has turned a corner, positioning itself so that it may be able to turn the corner at some point in the future.

So, if the company won’t be turning a profit anytime soon, then why am I writing about the stock now? In short, high volatility in shares has made selling options on LCID appear highly attractive. By selling far out-of-the-money put options, investors can generate an annualized yield of ~25% while taking very little risk.

With this trade, ideally, investors would not be assigned shares of LCID.

That said, if the stock does drop by half (or more) over the next 93 days, then getting in at a valuation closer to industry peers could make sense, especially given the strong top-line growth rate.

Today, I’ll dive deep into LCID, touch on the valuation, and explain why I believe selling put options on Lucid is the best way to earn returns in the stock, at least for now.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start with the basics.

As I mentioned before, Lucid Motors is an electric vehicle manufacturer that produces two models, the Lucid Air and the Lucid Gravity. The Lucid Air is a premium sedan, and the Lucid Gravity is an SUV:

Historically, the company has primarily manufactured the Lucid Air, but roughly one year ago it began producing and selling the Lucid Gravity to customers in the US, Europe, and the Middle East.

Let’s begin with the bad.

As you can likely imagine, Lucid is not currently profitable in any shape or form. The company is bleeding red all over the income statement, and sports negative gross, operating, and net margins of 97%, 297%, and 214%, respectively:

The reason that net margins are actually better than operating margins is due to the interest LCID gets paid on its cash pile.

If you know anything about car manufacturing, you’ll know that Lucid’s financial trajectory mirrors other vehicle startups in recent years - a period of heavy losses and investment, and the hope for eventually positive unit economics and net profits. This is the typical trajectory, because vehicle manufacturing is a capital-intensive industry and operationally difficult from an execution standpoint. Coordinating manufacturing supply chains, marketing, and more across continents, regulatory regimes, etc., is no easy task.

Thus, LCID is clearly in the investment stage of growth, and isn’t primed to deliver returns to investors anytime soon.

Now, the good.

In recent quarters, the company has begun delivering significant numbers of vehicles, producing anywhere between 2.2k and 3.8K vehicles per quarter, steadily growing deliveries sequentially over the last year:

As production has ramped, so too has revenue, with sales up 68% year over year:

As consumer awareness of the brand has improved, both through seeing Lucid vehicles on the street, as well as targeted brand campaigns featuring prominent celebrities like Timothée Chalamet, orders are moving up and to the right, showing that there is significant product-market fit here:

That is, as the company continues to make more and more cars, I expect there will be willing buyers at the price points the company is targeting. More broadly, a significant market exists for the types of vehicles Lucid has rolling off the production line:

In Q3, we accelerated our focus on building the Lucid brand and increasing awareness among luxury EV contenders, and we are already seeing results.

In the United States, brand awareness jumped 8 points month-over-month among consumers who plan to purchase an EV.

Shifting gears, Lucid also recently partnered with Uber (UBER) and Nuro to develop a new robotaxi offering. In this partnership, Lucid supplies the cars, Nuro supplies the tech, and Uber supplies the marketplace. This robotaxi service is set to start in the Bay Area this year, and this partnership also includes a $300 million capital injection from Uber into Lucid, further shoring up the company’s finances:

[W]e announced in September that we closed a $300 million strategic investment from Uber as part of our partnership to deploy 20,000 robotaxis or more. We already reached a first milestone with successfully delivering the first batch of robotaxi engineering vehicles to Nuro. Nuro is now finalizing their integration and will begin further testing activities. And we announced last week that the San Francisco Bay Area has been chosen as the first location for deployment planned in 2026.

As management remarked on a recent earnings call, it’s difficult to know where the robotaxi landscape will ultimately land; as in, will it become a premium service, or more commoditized?

[W]e are continuing our in-house development, but with a smart and resource-efficient approach. In early Q3, we already rolled out hands-free highway driving for the Lucid Air via OTA, and we will bring the same to the Gravity soon, fully developed in-house. The verdict is still out on whether owning autonomous driving technology in-house will be a sustainable differentiator or will become commoditized in the long run. Working with our partners allows us to monitor how the technology develops and make focused in-sourcing decisions once the future path becomes clear and avoid costly investments.

Either way, though, the capital injection from Uber and vehicle orders from Nuro will have a positive impact on the company and create long-term optionality.

Finally, in terms of runway, Lucid is in a great spot with roughly $3.8 billion in usable current assets on the balance sheet, and significant additional unused funding sources that it can draw upon, like the DDTL from Saudi Arabia’s PIF.

Interestingly, the company also has a relatively small long-term debt load of $2 billion. Thus, the company expects that it will be able to fund operations at the current burn rate well through the first half of 2027, a projection that I wholeheartedly agree with.

On the funding side, there’s one big positive and one big negative.

The big positive is that Saudi Arabia’s sovereign wealth fund appears invested in Lucid for the long haul. With a deep-pocketed nation backing your commercial ambitions, I would find it difficult to believe that LCID will ever truly starve for capital. There’s also a bit of a sunk cost fallacy here for the PIF; clearly, the company wants to own a significant portion of a next-generation EV manufacturer, and every time they put more capital in, it seems less and less likely that they’ll back away from supporting LCID.

On the negative side, however, LCID has also funded itself primarily through the use of share sales, and since coming public in 2021 the diluted share count has grown from 121 million to roughly 312 million:

While investors tend to be OK with moderate dilution - especially in the case of stock-based compensation - dilution like this materially harms current investors. This is because it pushes the share price of the stock down, even if the market cap remains the same, thus acting as a significant drag on any potential appreciation.

This is part of the reason that shares are down so much.

However, the good news here is that management is relatively flush with cash and has tended to sell shares into the market opportunistically. With shares trading where they are, I don’t expect considerable ongoing dilution, at least over the short and medium term.

Thus, when you take it all together, you’ve got a well-financed, cash-burning EV manufacturer that produces great cars and has inked an exciting partnership with Uber and a leading AV company. Sure, margins are negative, but production, deliveries, and revenues are all trending in the right direction , and - as the industry comes through a difficult period - the company’s results may get a material uplift.

Valuation

This all brings us to the valuation.

Right now, valuing shares of Lucid is particularly difficult because the company doesn’t make any money. Established industry veterans like Honda (HMC), Toyota (TM), General Motors (GM), and Ford (F) all trade with a price-to-sales ratio between ~0.3x and ~1x sales:

Thus, when viewed purely through that lens, Lucid, with a price-to-sales ratio of roughly 3x, appears 3 to 6 times as expensive as these other companies.

That said, Lucid is also growing rapidly, and with the partnership with Uber and continued backing by Saudi Arabia, the company does appear positioned well for the future. In cases like this, it’s extremely difficult to get a sense of what Lucid’s fair value should be, but from an investing standpoint, ideally I would like to buy shares of LCID at a much lower price that is more in line with industry peers.

The Trade

Thus, I see selling put options on shares of LCID as the best way to generate returns with the stock.

As a baseline, I don’t believe the company will be profitable anytime in the next 24 months. Thus, purely from a risk standpoint, ideally, this trade will not result in being assigned shares in LCID.

However, right now, in my view, selling May put options with a five dollar strike price provides an adequate margin of safety:

On the upside, put sellers at this price generate a significant cash-on-cash premium of 6.3% percent in the next 93 days, which annualizes to a yield of 25%. If LCID can stay above $5 over the next 93 days, then this is a significantly better yield than you can find in virtually all other instruments, including bonds, closed-end funds, BDCs, MLPs, etc.

On the downside, in the event that shares dip over the next three months, investors could be assigned shares of LCID with a cost basis around ~$4.70.

This implies a price-to-sales ratio of roughly 1.4x.

This isn't ideal, as it's still a higher-than-average price vs. scaled automaker peers, but it's also a hell of a lot cheaper than you're getting right now on the open market. Thus, unlike other put-selling trades that I’ve written about here on Seeking Alpha, I don’t see this one as being a potential win-win, but I still believe it's a +expected value trade.

Additionally, it's important to note that the company expects to report earnings in the next few weeks, which could cause additional volatility, potentially sending shares beneath the put option strike price. In the event of being assigned shares in the mid-single digits, there's some valuation and execution risk associated with the stock.

Summary

That said, I believe in LCID’s long-term outlook as it hopefully begins to rein in spending and get its unit economics in order.

Entering the stock at the current price appears too soon, and even by selling puts, an assignment in the mid-single digits might still be a bit risky.

However, the significant cash-on-cash yield produced by these options and the robust balance sheet situation should give investors a significant enough margin of safety to have confidence here.

Hence, my hold rating.

Cheers~

data.title: Advanced Drainage Systems Is Doing Great, But That Doesn't Mean Gains Deserve To Flow
data.description:
The last few months have been really good time for shareholders of Advanced Drainage Systems (WMS). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate back in September of 2025, the stock has shot up 20.9%. That is significantly greater than the 5.3% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. This comes at a time when revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all rising. What's more, management seems optimistic about the future, and the company recently completed a sizable acquisition that should push revenue and some cash flow metrics even higher. As great as the company is from an operational standpoint, however, it's not really easy to get behind at the moment. Unfortunately, the stock looks pretty solidly fairly valued, both on an absolute basis and relative to companies that have similarities to it. As as much as I would love to turn bullish on it right now, I don't see that being a realistic and fruitful path.

Solid results and big moves

Just the other day, on February 5th, the management team at Advanced Drainage Systems announced financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue for the quarter totaled $693.4 million. Although this was up only a hair compared to the $690.5 million the company reported a year earlier, it did exceed what analysts anticipated to the tune of $9 million. This was not the only improvement that the company saw. Earnings per share popped from $1.04 to $1.19. In fact, the earnings reported by the company ended up being $0.10 greater than what had been anticipated. This meant a rise in net profits from $81.2 million to $93.6 million. Operating cash flow for the business expanded from $190 million to $269.3 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $143.5 million to $164.5 million. And finally, EBITDA grew from $191.5 million to $209.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. As was the case with the third quarter on its own, revenue, profits, and cash flows, increased for the business. This is part of a pretty consistent trend for the company. However, it is worth noting that not all parts of the company are equal.

During the most recent quarter, two of the three operating segments for the business actually reported declining revenue. The larger of these is its Pipe segment, which is responsible for around 47.1% to the company’s overall sales. Year over year, revenue actually dropped 3.8% from $339.6 million to $326.7 million. This drop, according to the company, can be attributed to lower sales volumes in the residential and infrastructure end markets. To be honest with you, the weakness in the residential side is not shocking to me in the least bit. I have long been talking about weakness in the residential market. The most recent data that we have regarding housing was published in early January and covers October of last year. Housing completions were down 15.3% year over year. Housing starts had dropped 7.8%. Building permits were down 1.1%. Non-residential construction activity ended up dropping about 0.9% year over year for the month of October. And that doesn't even factor in public construction. However, that did see a slight increase from $513.4 billion to $524 billion.

Even though this was the big pain point for the company, the other segment that saw a decline was the International segment. Revenue dipped 2.5% from $50.4 million to $49.1 million. Management did not really reveal much here. But given the weakness that other countries are experiencing when it comes to inflationary pressures and a broader economic malaise that has formed, this doesn't surprise me all that much. The good news is that the other operating segments for the company made up for this.

To a lesser extent, the Infiltrator segment did well, with revenue climbing 1.9% from $150 million to $152.9 million. This, according to the company, was the result of higher demand in the residential market as the need for tanks and treatment products expanded. But even more impressive ended up being the Allied Products & Other segment. Revenue jumped an impressive 9.4% from $150.5 million to $160.7 million. Despite weakness in the residential market and elsewhere, this segment benefited from market share gains that the company was able to capture, benefits from the introduction of new products, and programs dedicated to servicing customers and boosting overall distribution. Examples of key products that did well here ended up being its StormTech storage chambers and its Nyloplast capture structures. Water quality products also did well. And all of this was because of new product introductions at the company achieved in the prior 12 months. It also helps that the firm was aided by more favorable multifamily construction activity.

When it comes to 2026 as a whole, management said that revenue should come in at between $2.99 billion and $3.04 billion. For context, in 2025, revenue was $2.90 billion. From this revenue, the company expects EBITDA to range between $930 million and $960 million. Last year, it stood at $889.2 million. In all likelihood, hitting the midpoint of expectations would mean net profits of about $475.1 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $744 million. Both of these represent increases over what the company achieved in 2025.

Normally, I would go on to value the company on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. But we do need to address the 800 pound elephant in the room. Last year, management announced that they had agreed to acquire National Diversified Sales, which is a water management business owned by Norma Group SE. I absolutely love anything that has to do with the water industry because of my own views regarding water scarcity and how important an issue it will be in the decades to come. Management views this as a highly complementary transaction because of its focus on the residential water management, access box, and irrigation solutions markets. But this is not a small purchase. Management agreed to pay $1 billion for the business. But net of tax benefits, that price is a bit lower at $875 million.

All combined, this newly acquired business generates revenue of around $313 million. About 55% of those sales come from stormwater management activities. Landscape irrigation is responsible for 30%. And the remaining 15% involves flow management services. The vast majority of the company's revenue comes from the Americas. But about 10% of sales come from the Asia Pacific region and EMEA (Europe, the Middle East, and Africa). The overall revenue acquired should bring in annual EBITDA of around $62 million. But if everything goes according to plan, within three years of the completion of the transaction, management expects to capture about $25 million in run rate synergies.

In the table above, you can see calculations that I've performed to arrive at the impact to net profits and operating cash flow that should come from this purchase. I did have to make assumptions in this case, such as those involving tax rates, depreciation, and overall interest expense associated with the rise in net debt. But I believe that these assumptions are all reasonable. Without synergies, we are looking at a slight decline in overall net profits. Cash flows should rise slightly. But with synergies, the picture does look a bit more favorable.

In the chart above, you can see how Advanced Drainage Systems is valued under a few different scenarios. The first scenario looks at how the business is valued using projections for 2026 and results from 2025 without this purchase factored in. The second scenario looks at the purchase being completed, but without any synergies captured. And the third scenario looks at the purchase having been completed with synergies fully achieved. The overall trading multiples don't change drastically. At the end of the day, this means that the picture is relatively low risk for the business. And the fact that the firm does look more attractive under the assumption that synergies are completed means that we are looking at a favorable risk to reward opportunity here.

As part of my analysis, I then compared Advanced Drainage Systems to five other similar enterprises. In addition to the stock looking pretty much fairly valued on an absolute basis, it also looks fairly valued compared to these other enterprises as well. Across the board, two of the five competitors are cheaper than it is, which essentially puts it around the middle of the group in terms of valuation.

Takeaway

As much as I want to be bullish about Advanced Drainage Systems right now, I can't. Even with this exciting acquisition that was just completed, and even with management forecasting rosy results for 2026 compared to 2025, the picture looks decent, but not great. As things stand, the best thing I can do is to maintain my ‘hold’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Inflection Points: Earning Proof Points
data.description:
The last month felt like a year. Halfway through the first earnings season of 2026, and markets have already experienced two volatility spikes.1 The first followed the nomination of Kevin Warsh as Federal Reserve chair and hotter-than-expected producer inflation. The second emerged as mega-cap doubled down on AI investment, stoking fears that firms may struggle to deliver the expected growth and profitability. That concern is likely overdone, as AI integration at the enterprise and consumer agentic levels remains in the early innings.2

Good news is that the breadth of companies participating in the productivity boom is improving and delivering results. Large cap companies have consistently beaten expectations this earnings season and generally revised guidance higher, breaking from historical norms.3 Parsing the data to contextualize recent events helps explain the market rotation and hints at themes to consider. It also provides reassurance that the backdrop remains favorable for risk assets, including popular themes like AI and emerging areas such as materials tied to energy and infrastructure.

A Fundamentals-Driven Market

At the start of 2026, rate markets were pricing in two cuts for the year, but expectations for a second cut have declined.4 The Warsh nomination coinciding with a bout of equity volatility reopened the question whether this market is fundamentals- or rates-driven. The market’s initial read labeled Warsh as a hawk less likely to lower rates in the near term, but we believe that might be too simplistic of a view.5

The hawk–dove narrative misses the broader point: Warsh is likely to take a holistic view on economic growth. For years, the former Fed governor has advocated for policies that would promote economic growth through investment and productivity.6 Lower rates could help accelerate investment, suggesting the probability of two or three cuts remains reasonably high. That said, Warsh would likely look to offset the inflationary pressure from rate cuts through balance sheet reduction, effectively unraveling quantitative easing (QE) policies. He has been critical of QE in the past, noting that the Fed’s intervention impedes market-determined asset prices and investment.7

In theory, lower rates combined with a reduced Fed presence in markets would be a pro-growth mix. Of course, reducing the $6.6 trillion balance sheet is easier said than done and could drive rates higher as the supply of securities increases.8 Deregulation of the financial system, and specifically lower capital requirements for banks, could free up capacity to absorb the supply and minimize the impact on rates.

Despite the volatility around Warsh’s nomination and the perceived rate impact, we view this market as fundamentals-driven. With the large-cap cost of capital still relatively high, lower rates can lift valuations, but that may just be the icing on the cake.9 Equity markets advanced on strong fundamentals in 2025, and we believe that trend is likely to persist.

In 2025, all S&P 500 price gains were realized during the 16 weeks of earnings season, while returns for the remaining 36 weeks were negative.10 Investors responded to positive earnings news, and in between proved patient during a year of elevated monetary, fiscal, and geopolitical policy uncertainty.

The S&P 500 advanced approximately 16% in 2025, driven by 14% earnings growth.11 At year-end 2024, the index traded at 21.5 times 2025 estimated earnings. By year-end 2025, the multiple had expanded by just a half point to 22 times 2026E earnings.12 The gains were driven not by exuberance or valuation expansion, but by strong corporate performance.

While the Fed lowered rates three times, the cuts did not translate to a lower cost of capital.13 Almost immediately after the Fed started cutting rates 18 months ago, long rates started moving higher, and valuation is sensitive to long rather short rates. During that span, 10-year yields were up 25 basis points, while the 30-year gained 75 basis points.14 The weighted average cost of capital for S&P 500 constituents actually rose from 10.3% to 10.7% in 2025. Equities may have benefited from the expectation of lower rates, but it is hard to argue that equities rose because the cost of capital declined.

Meanwhile, economic conditions improved. GDP growth forecasts have been off the mark since 2023 with actuals consistently coming in better than expected. In 2025, GDP growth in the second and third quarters were roughly 50 basis points better than forecasted.15 Expectations for 2026 have followed a similar pattern with estimates now moving higher. At the start of 2026, the estimate for real GDP was 2.0%, and has since improved to 2.4%. Nominal GDP growth for the year ahead is now around 5.1%, meaningfully better than the 4.3% forecast at the May 2025 trough.16

Clues in Earnings Guidance

The company performance bar is high at the start of 2026. S&P 500 companies are forecast to grow revenues by 8% and earnings by 15%, with profit margins near record levels. Since 1992, S&P 500 companies have averaged 4% revenue growth and 9% EPS growth, making 2026 expectations an outlier.17 The risk embedded in current market pricing is that companies fail to meet or exceed estimates.

Earnings season gives companies an opportunity to refine guidance based on current data and potential scenarios for the year ahead. Interestingly, one-third of the way through this earnings season, most companies have raised their 2026 guidance. Of the 170 that had reported as of January 30, revenue estimates increased by 1%, EPS rose 0.5%, and 60% raised full-year guidance.18 That represents a solid setup.

Every S&P 500 sector has delivered positive earnings surprises thus far amid strong growth across most sectors. Tech and communication services profits have been strong, though industrials and materials lead the way.19 Financials, utilities, and energy have also delivered respectable year-over-year earnings performance. Importantly, growth and revisions aren’t limited to the Magnificent 7; a broader set of companies and industries are performing well. While staples, materials, healthcare, and discretionary have not, in aggregate, delivered positive profit growth thus far, all have posted upside surprises relative to prior forecasts and solid revenue growth.20

Bullish revisions to guidance are something of a historical anomaly. Since 1991, actual corporate earnings have typically come in 6% lower than the forecast to start the year, as investors begin with rosy expectations that are later revised to reflect economic reality.21 There is, however, a notable break in the data. Prior to 2015, the average deviation between forecast and actual S&P 500 earnings was 9% lower. Over the past decade, this relationship has changed, with actual earnings averaging 2% above expectations set 12 months earlier.22 The current revisions are therefore in line with more recent experience and suggest that actual earnings could still surprise to the upside.

Early January revisions are a positive sign for the year ahead. Historically, in years when S&P 500 earnings estimates are raised in January, full-year estimates also tend to move higher. Excluding the recessionary periods of 1991–1993 and 2001, as well as the post-pandemic economic snapback in 2020, there is a strong statistical correlation between January revisions and full-year performance.23 A 1% increase to January forecasts has historically translated into full-year earnings coming in about 2% above expectations, suggesting there may still be room for upside surprises despite already lofty expectations for 2026.

Market Rotation and Signs of Economic Broadening

The question we get a lot: when will the market broaden out? Mega-cap dominance, led by tech and communication services, has concentrated cap weights; however, a real economic broadening may be driving some of the market moves in early 2026.

While the primary equity indexes started 2026 higher, a rotation began in late January and carried into February.24 Former market darlings in software and data services fell, while commodities and energy outperformed. The contrast has been stark: the top-performing Bloomberg industry, commodity chemicals, is outperforming the weakest, application software, by 60% year-to-date as of February 4.25

S&P 500 factor performance tells a similar story. Liquidity, fundamentals, and size led the market higher in 2025, while the leading factors in January 2026 were momentum, liquidity, and dividend yield.26 Growth, quality, and size lagged. This rotation could be a knee-jerk reaction or a sign that other parts of the economy may be well positioned to improve productivity, leading to broader participation.

Evidence of broader economic participation is already working into expectations. From 2023 to 2025, the Mag 7 delivered nearly 25% annualized earnings growth, compared with less than 5% for the other 493 companies.27 That gap is forecast to narrow significantly over the next two years, with Mag 7 EPS growth slowing to 13% and the rest of the index accelerating to 11%.

While we have long expected productivity gains to emerge beyond big tech, fundamentals are still likely to win out, and the fundamentals for tech and growth equities remain strong. For example, the S&P 500 Software & Services Index is priced at 23.4 times 2026E earnings, supported by revenue and EPS growth of 19% and profit margins of 30%.28 That valuation still looks attractive relative to S&P 500 revenue growth of 8% and profit margins of 15% at 22 times 2026E earnings.

Rather than tossing the baby out with the bathwater, fundamentals can help navigate a prolonged shift in economic participation. Companies tied to the AI ecosystem are still likely to generate strong cash flow, but the onset of the Automation Age also likely creates opportunities in electrification, commodities, and industrials like infrastructure and defense.

At this stage of the AI buildout, efficiency is an operative word. The major AI players making big investments face a prove-it moment when monetization becomes critical, though much of this pressure is embedded in expectations. Over the next two years, earnings per dollar of capital expenditure for the Mag 7 is expected to decline but rise for the other 493 companies.29 By 2027, these companies are forecast to be roughly twice as efficient as the Mag 7 at converting corporate investment into earnings.

The efficiency angle also appears in expectations for profit margin improvement across sectors over the next two years.30 The best of both worlds may be achieved by owning the AI infrastructure and service providers alongside companies finding efficiency gains. The goal is to benefit from growth and profitability today while participating in the ongoing productivity and profitability shifts likely to emerge in the years ahead.

Footnotes

  1. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  2. McKinsey & Company. (2025, November 5). The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation. The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation
  3. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  4. Ibid.
  5. Helfstein, S. (2026, February 3). Hearing Warsh: Growth Along the Watchtower. Global X. Global X ETFs
  6. Federal Reserve. (2010, November 2-3). Meeting of the Federal Open Market Committee. https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/files/FOMC20101103meeting.pdf.
  7. Ibid.
  8. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  9. Ibid.
  10. Ibid.
  11. Ibid.
  12. Ibid.
  13. Ibid.
  14. Ibid.
  15. Ibid.
  16. Ibid.
  17. Ibid.
  18. Ibid.
  19. Ibid.
  20. Ibid.
  21. Ibid.
  22. Ibid.
  23. Ibid.
  24. Ibid.
  25. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on February 4, 2026.
  26. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  27. Ibid.
  28. Ibid.
  29. Ibid.
  30. Ibid.

Information provided by Global X Management Company LLC.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

This material represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific point in time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, or a guarantee of future results. This information is not intended to be individual or personalized investment or tax advice and should not be used for trading purposes. Please consult a financial advisor or tax professional for more information regarding your investment and/or tax situation.

Original Post

data.title: U.K. GDP Underwhelms With 0.1% Growth In Q4, FTSE 100 Slips And GBP/USD Advances
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Preliminary data from the ONS indicates that the UK economy expanded by 0.1% in the final quarter of 2025, maintaining the same growth rate as the third quarter but falling just short of the 0.2% forecast by analysts.

This sluggish performance was marked by a significant divergence across sectors. The production industry provided a strong tailwind, surging 1.2% to recover from a previous decline, with manufacturing specifically rising 0.9% as automotive production stabilized following a major cyber disruption in August.

However, these gains were largely neutralized by a flatlining services sector, where business-facing activities stalled and consumer-facing services grew by a marginal 0.2%.

Furthermore, the construction sector acted as a significant drag on the economy, contracting by 2.1% and reversing its modest growth from the prior period.

The initial market reaction

Markets seemed to shrug off the lackluster data, with both GBP/USD and the FTSE 100 on the offensive this morning.

The FTSE 100 has since fallen around 50 points from the fresh all-time highs, while GBP/USD eyes a move higher in the European session.

GBP/USD movement may be driven in part by the US dollar and its performance today ahead of the CPI data tomorrow.

The big picture

When examining the broader economic landscape, the UK's GDP grew by 1.0% in the final quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024. This steady annual performance was characterized by balanced contributions across the board, with both the services and production sectors expanding by 1.0%, while construction saw a more modest increase of 0.2%.

By the end of the year, December 2025 specifically showed a 0.7% improvement in economic output over the previous December, signaling a consistent, albeit measured, upward trend.

Taking the full year into account, the UK economy expanded by 1.3% in 2025 relative to 2024. This growth was primarily fueled by a 1.4% rise in services and a robust 1.8% increase in construction output.

Notably, the production sector returned to positive territory with a 0.2% increase, marking its first year of annual growth since 2021. Overall, these figures suggest a year of gradual recovery and the breaking of a multi-year stagnation in industrial output.

Is the lack of service sector growth a concern?

The services sector remained stagnant in the final quarter of 2025, recording zero growth in the three months leading up to December compared to the previous quarter.

This flat performance marks a continued trend of inertia, as recent data revisions have erased previously reported gains; growth for the periods ending in both October and November was adjusted downward to 0.0%.

Consequently, this latest update confirms a third consecutive month of stagnation for the UK's dominant economic sector on a rolling three-month basis.

While "zero growth" sounds alarming for a sector that makes up roughly 80% of the UK economy, the situation has been attributed to a mix of structural concern and temporary "Budget indigestion."

It does remain a concern though with the UK so dependent on services, the economy cannot achieve meaningful growth if this sector is flat. In Q4, the overall GDP only managed a tiny 0.1% increase, and that was largely thanks to a rebound in manufacturing.

One of the factors playing a role is the November 2025 budget. The increase in National Insurance contributions for employers and the rise in the minimum wage caused many service-based firms to freeze hiring and investment as they recalculated their costs.

In conclusion

It is a significant concern for the government's growth targets, as it shows the economy is currently walking a tightrope. However, many analysts remain optimistic, as the early 2026 data suggests it may be a "stumble" rather than a "fall."

Original Post

data.title: Astera's Pause Before The Second Surge
data.description:
A good quarter by Astera Labs (ALAB) but the stock move was based on expectation rather than reality. The stock went down because the investors were looking for acceleration, not normalization. The unexpected CFO change adds a layer of complexity to the growth story. The guidance was a little weaker than expected due to mix effects and investment. Operating costs are increasing as they move into bigger opportunities.

My thesis in December was around Astera’s structural change to become a scale-up control point with the view that Scorpio X would deliver a breakout year in 2026 based on increasing content per rack and hyperscaler engagement. What’s changed is the timing and the expectations. The growth rate is slowing down, the margins are being reset and Scorpio X’s acceleration is back half weighted, making it a story about execution.

A Strong Quarter Meets a Nervous Market

The fourth quarter was one of those quarters where beating expectations doesn’t even require an excuse. Revenue was $270.6 million, up 17% sequentially and up 92% year over year. Revenue for the year came in at $852.5 million, up 115%. Non-GAAP gross margin was again high at 75.7%. Operating cash flows were $95.3 million, leaving the company with $1.19 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

However, the stock move quickly shifted from the good quarter to the guidance. Revenue for the first quarter is expected to be $286 million to $297 million. This is still growth but only 6% to 10% sequentially. Gross margin is expected to be around 74%, while operating costs increase meaningfully. This is a near-term reset in the margin profile, and I can see why investors are nervous.

Where Protocol Wars Meet Platform Power

The call was dominated by questions about UALink, NVLink Fusion, Ethernet scale up, and ESUN. My interpretation is that Ethernet is optimized for scale out while PCI Express is optimized for low latency attachment and memory level workflows within the rack. Scale up is where the architecture becomes personal, and hyperscalers follow their software stack rather than a standard.

This is the reason I continue to focus on Astera’s core strength. Astera is not just providing a piece part. Astera is qualifying itself as the connectivity piece for memory centric designs and then leveraging that to go through multiple generations. If they are going to be important, they have the ability to design into that, but the opportunity today is around PCI Express, UALink, and custom scale up.

Scorpio X: The Real Acceleration Engine

Scorpio P is already in volume but Scorpio X is the product that sets the pace for the next acceleration. It has modest contributions in the first half of 2026, a transition to high volumes in the second half of 2026, and more ramping in 2027. That’s why the growth in the first quarter looks so benign compared to the double-digit growth in the previous quarters.

What makes Scorpio X interesting is not the size, it’s the positioning. Scale up fabrics have become more customized, and Astera is trying to leverage silicon while differentiating on software-defined features and system-level integration. When they get it right, they can serve more topologies without having to do a new tape out.

Another thing I’m looking at is the mix within Scorpio. The P series is scale out, the X series is scale up, where the dollars per rack can be richer. And so, if the X can ramp as they expect, they can grow the company even as Aries and Taurus normalize from the surge in 2025.

Winning on Both Sides of the AI Divide

Astera is doubling down on the opportunity with UALink as an open, high performance scale-up protocol, with public customer roadmaps calling out to 2027 ramp times. At the same time, Astera is calling out NVLink Fusion as an add-on opportunity, with Astera taking their native protocol and converting it to NVLink, attaching to hybrid racks on a one-to-one basis per accelerator. The revenue opportunity, according to management, has the potential to be similar to selling a switched fabric, as the attach rates can help offset the lower economics of the sale.

I like this positioning as it removes the risk of choosing the wrong ecosystem. In this industry with hyperscalers building their own custom accelerators and still deploying Nvidia-based solutions, Astera can be relevant to both, as long as they stay within the custom path and maintain their higher content.

Amazon’s Warrant: Trust & Accounting Noise

Astera filed their warrant agreement related to Amazon purchases, which can result in purchases of up to $6.5 billion related to smart fabric switch, signal conditioning and optical engine products. This is not an easy agreement to make, and it speaks volumes to the trust and confidence that Amazon has in Astera, as well as their desire to incent their partners over time.

The issue of course is accounting, as the company will incur a non-cash expense as tranches vest, which will effectively lower revenue and gross margins, with management calling out a two-point gross margin reduction starting in the second quarter. In other words, their margins will be lower just as this relationship is starting to prove itself.

A Valuation Premium That Demands Perfection

ALAB is a company that is trading at a valuation which assumes flawless execution. If we look at trailing numbers, the company is trading at over 40x sales and over 100x non-GAAP earnings, while the sector averages are in the mid-20s on the earnings side and low single digits on the sales side.

Even if we look at forward numbers ALAB trades at a forward Price/Sales of 22x and an even higher multiple on the forward Price/Earnings. The consensus estimate is that the company will earn around $2.54 per share in 2026 and $3.44 per share in 2027 which is clearly going to be a growth curve, but the current valuation already assumes a lot of that growth.

The company is at the center of the AI connectivity universe and has increasing content per rack, which is why the premium exists. The problem is that the valuation leaves no room for any issues with the ramp of the Scorpio X product, any issues with hyperscaler spend and any issues with margin volatility. The stock is not a value play at this point but a growth play with a long duration and assumes acceleration.

4 Risks the Market Can’t Ignore

The first risk is protocol drift. Should the scale-up architectures standardize along the path where Astera is not included, the ramps may not materialize as expected.

The second risk is timing. The Scorpio X has lengthy qualification cycles. Any slippage in the timeline may pressure the stock as it is valued for second-half acceleration. The third risk is competition. The retimers, cables, and switches have aggressive competitor activity and internal designs from large customers.

And lastly the fourth risk is optical timing risk. The 2028 expectations for co-packaged optics may cause sentiment to accelerate ahead of the revenue realization.

Bottom Line

I’m not worried about the one-quarter margin move but about remaining embedded in the custom rack solution. Scorpio X represents the second growth wave, and the Amazon warrant proves that there’s real pull from the customers, even if it’s a distortion in the gross margin picture in the short term. The 2027s ramping as planned makes the current slowdown appear to be a consolidation rather than a breakdown.
